We were proud of our JWT setup. Stateless, scalable, modern. We had read all the right articles, watched the right conference talks, and done everything exactly the way the internet told us to.
Then our users started getting logged out randomly. Password resets stopped working. One guy opened three support tickets in a single day, each one saying some version of the same thing — he was “logged in but also not logged in.”
We spent four hours on that one ticket. We decoded his token. We checked the expiry. Everything looked valid on paper.
But the user was sitting there, locked out of his account, furious, and we were staring at a perfectly signed JWT that we could not invalidate, could not reason about, and could not fix without a full redeployment.
That was the moment I realized we had built ourselves a very modern, very elegant trap.
The JWT Promise Vs. The JWT Reality
Everyone sells JWT as the clean solution. No database lookups. No session store. Just encode the user id, sign it, ship it to the client, and let the token carry all the truth.
But here is what nobody tells you when you are setting it up following a tutorial: JWT tokens, once issued…
